desc: Zermatt is a high-alpine village beneath the Matterhorn, combining world-class skiing with exceptional summer hiking in a car-free mountain setting. It is one of the Alps' premium year-round destinations, with strong winter ski demand, busy midsummer hiking windows, and consistently high accommodation pricing across peak holiday periods.

todo.name: Gornergrat Railway
todo.desc: Reserve early morning departures in peak seasons for clearer Matterhorn views, and buy combined rail-hike tickets if you plan to descend on foot.

todo.name: Matterhorn Glacier Paradise
todo.desc: Book the first cable-car wave on stable-weather days, and carry warm layers year-round because summit wind chill is severe even in summer.

todo.name: Five Lakes Walk (5-Seenweg)
todo.desc: This route is best from late June to September once snow patches clear; start before 09:00 for calmer lakes and fewer bottlenecks on narrow sections.

todo.name: Sunnegga & Rothorn area
todo.desc: Check lift status at breakfast because wind holds are common; in winter prebook ski-school slots and in summer pair lifts to avoid long descents.

todo.name: Zermatt old village
todo.desc: Use this as a low-weather backup block: museums and historic lanes are walkable from station hotels when high lifts are storm-affected.

todo.name: Ski the Theodul Glacier
todo.desc: Aim for early runs when snow is firmest, and track Italy-side connection times if you intend to cross lifts toward Cervinia.

todo.name: Mountain hut lunch
todo.desc: Reserve terrace tables on bluebird weekends and carry cash backup, as some smaller huts have limited card connectivity in poor weather.

todo.name: Charles Kuonen Suspension Bridge day trip
todo.desc: Reach the trailhead by train to Randa, then allow 4-6 hours round trip with alpine footwear; avoid this hike during thunderstorm forecasts.

wifi.note: Generally strong in hotels and apartments; café reliability is good but workspace and food costs are high.

prac.fltNote: No direct London departures; Typical access is via Geneva (GVA) or Zurich (ZRH), then rail via Visp to car-free Zermatt (about 3.5-4.5 hours transfer). Return flight fares are often ~£70-£190 off-peak, ~£190-£320 in shoulder demand, and ~£320+ around ski and summer peaks.
prac.lang: German (Swiss German); English widely used in tourism
prac.visa: Switzerland is in Schengen; UK and US passport holders can generally stay visa-free up to 90 days in any 180-day period.
alerts: Zermatt is car-free, so arrivals require train plus short transfer from Täsch; pack light enough for station-to-hotel walks or prebook electric taxi.
Ski holidays (Christmas-New Year, February half-term, Easter) and prime July-August hiking weeks often need 3-6 months accommodation lead time.
High-alpine lifts and trails can close at short notice for wind, lightning, or avalanche control, so check morning operations reports before committing to routes.

sweet: Late January-March is usually best for ski conditions, while July-September is strongest for open trails and long alpine days.
